lessons.title: 1.1 The Claimants to the Throne
narrative: In January 1066, King Edward the Confessor died without leaving a direct heir. This sparked a succession crisis as three main claimants competed for the English throne:
1. **Harold Godwinson**, the Earl of Wessex: Crowned immediately as King Harold II with the support of the English council (the Witan).
2. **William**, Duke of Normandy: Claimed Edward had promised him the throne in 1051, and that Harold Godwinson had sworn a sacred oath to support his claim.
3. **Harald Hardrada**, the King of Norway: A Viking warrior who claimed the throne based on an old agreement between his predecessor and the former Danish rulers of England.

tasks.text: Who was crowned King of England immediately after Edward the Confessor's death?
Where was Duke William, one of the primary claimants, from?

vocab.definition: The order or process of inheriting a title, office, or throne.
A person who asserts a right to a title or throne.
The council of English nobles and bishops who advised the Anglo-Saxon kings.

teacher_notes.primer: Teacher primer for this lesson has not been added yet. Use the breakdown below to guide your teaching.

objectives.primer: Teacher notes for this lesson have not been added yet.

learning_objectives.overarching: To analyze the succession crisis triggered by the death of Edward the Confessor.
scaffolded: Identify the three main claimants to the English throne.
Explain the basis of each claimant's argument for the crown.
Evaluate which claimant had the most legitimate right to succeed Edward.

lessons.enquiry: Who had the strongest claim to the English throne in 1066?

lessons.title: 1.2 The Battle of Hastings
narrative: William of Normandy prepared an invasion fleet. At the same time, Harald Hardrada invaded the north of England, prompting Harold Godwinson to march north and defeat the Vikings at the Battle of Stamford Bridge.
While Harold was in the north, William landed his forces on the south coast at Pevensey. Harold rushed south, and the two armies met at Senlac Hill near Hastings on 14 October 1066.

The Anglo-Saxons formed a strong shield wall at the top of the hill. The Normans failed to break it until they used the tactic of the **feigned retreat**—pretending to run away to lure the Saxons down the hill. Once the shield wall broke, King Harold was killed, and the Normans emerged victorious.

tasks.text: What was the name of the military tactic where Normans pretended to retreat to break the Saxon shield wall?

vocab.definition: A defensive tactic where soldiers overlap shields to form a solid barrier.
A tactical withdrawal where soldiers pretend to flee to draw out defenders.

teacher_notes.primer: Teacher primer for this lesson has not been added yet. Use the breakdown below to guide your teaching.

objectives.primer: Teacher notes for this lesson have not been added yet.

learning_objectives.overarching: To evaluate the strategic, tactical, and lucky factors that led to the Norman Conquest.
scaffolded: Describe the composition and tactics of the Saxon and Norman armies.
Explain the key events of the battle, including the 'feigned retreats'.
Analyze the relative importance of William's leadership versus Harold's mistakes.

lessons.enquiry: Why was William of Normandy victorious at the Battle of Hastings?

biographies.name: Yitzhak Rabin
achievements: Chief of Staff of the IDF during the 1967 Six-Day War.
Signed the historic 1993 Oslo Accords with Yasser Arafat.
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1994.
biographies.significance: He transitioned from a military hardliner to a statesman willing to negotiate land for peace, opening the first real path to a two-state solution before his tragic assassination.

biographies.name: Yasser Arafat
biographies.role: Chairman of the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO)
achievements: Founded Fatah and led the PLO for decades.
Recognized Israel's right to exist in 1988, shifting the PLO's diplomatic stance.
Signed the 1993 Oslo Accords and became the first President of the Palestinian Authority.
biographies.significance: He was the globally recognized face of the Palestinian nationalist movement, leading the shift from armed struggle to diplomatic negotiations in the 1990s.

biographies.name: Bill Clinton
achievements: Brokered and hosted the signing of the 1993 Oslo Accords.
Hosted the 2000 Camp David Summit in an attempt to reach a final status agreement.
biographies.significance: He was the primary international mediator during the most optimistic phase of the peace process, investing massive American diplomatic capital in resolving the conflict.

biographies.name: Anwar Sadat
achievements: Led Egypt during the 1973 Yom Kippur War.
Became the first Arab leader to officially visit Israel (1977).
Signed the 1978 Camp David Accords, securing peace with Israel.
biographies.significance: His bold decision to break the Arab consensus and negotiate a bilateral peace treaty with Israel fundamentally shifted the military balance in the Middle East.

biographies.name: Menachem Begin
achievements: Former leader of the Irgun paramilitary group.
Signed the 1978 Camp David Accords with Egypt.
biographies.significance: As a right-wing leader, his willingness to return the Sinai Peninsula to Egypt proved that 'land for peace' could work, though his Lebanon policies were highly controversial.

biographies.name: Jimmy Carter
achievements: Championed human rights as a core pillar of US foreign policy.
biographies.significance: His relentless personal mediation at Camp David forced Sadat and Begin to compromise, resulting in the most durable peace treaty in the modern Middle East.

biographies.name: Ariel Sharon
biographies.role: Minister of Defence / Prime Minister of Israel
achievements: Led Israel during the Second Intifada.
Engineered the unilateral disengagement from Gaza in 2005.
biographies.significance: A fiercely controversial military tactician whose aggressive policies deeply shaped Israel's security architecture and settlement strategy.
